title: Priority Queues & Heaps
description: Heaps are the backbone of scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, and stream processing. Python's heapq is always a min-heap — knowing how to adapt it is a core FAANG skill.

sections.title: Heap Fundamentals & Complexity
sections.description: A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored as a flat array. For any node at index i: left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, parent at (i-1)//2. No pointer overhead — cache-friendly.
sections.codeBlock:
import heapq

# heapify — O(N) (not O(N log N) — uses sift-down, not N pushes)
heap = [40, 10, 30, 5, 15]
heapq.heapify(heap)
print(heap[0])          # 5 — always the minimum

# push — O(log N)
heapq.heappush(heap, 2)

# pop minimum — O(log N)
smallest = heapq.heappop(heap)   # 2

# pushpop — O(log N), faster than separate push+pop
result = heapq.heappushpop(heap, 1)  # pushes 1, pops minimum

# nlargest / nsmallest — O(N log K)
top3 = heapq.nlargest(3, [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6])  # [9, 6, 5]
# ponytail: use sorted()[-k:] for K close to N; heapq for small K

sections.title: Max-Heap & Custom Priority
sections.description: Python only supports min-heaps. For max-heap: negate values. For custom priority: push tuples — Python compares lexicographically. This is the standard FAANG interview technique.
sections.codeBlock:
import heapq

# Max-heap via negation
max_heap = []
for val in [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]:
    heapq.heappush(max_heap, -val)

print(-heapq.heappop(max_heap))  # 9 (max)

# Custom priority with tuples: (priority, item)
# Lower number = higher priority (min-heap semantics)
tasks = []
heapq.heappush(tasks, (3, 'low priority task'))
heapq.heappush(tasks, (1, 'urgent task'))
heapq.heappush(tasks, (2, 'medium task'))

priority, task = heapq.heappop(tasks)  # (1, 'urgent task')

# Tie-breaking: add a counter to avoid comparing non-comparable objects
import itertools
counter = itertools.count()
heapq.heappush(tasks, (1, next(counter), {'name': 'task_obj'}))  # never compares dicts

sections.title: Top-K Elements & K-Way Merge
sections.description:
Top-K pattern: Maintain a min-heap of size K while scanning. If current element > heap top, replace. Final heap contains the K largest. O(N log K) — much better than sort O(N log N) when K ≪ N.

K-Way Merge: Merge K sorted arrays efficiently. Push the first element of each array onto a min-heap, then repeatedly pop the min and push the next element from that array. O(N log K) where N = total elements.

sections.title: Median of a Data Stream — Two Heaps
sections.description: Classic FAANG hard problem. Maintain two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. Keep them balanced (sizes differ by at most 1). Median is either the top of the larger heap or the average of both tops. All operations O(log N).

sections.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm — Heap-Based
sections.description: Dijkstra's finds shortest paths from a source to all nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges. Using a min-heap gives O((V + E) log V). Always use heapq — the priority queue is the core of the algorithm.

details:
heapify is O(N), not O(N log N): This surprises many. Building a heap by repeatedly calling heappush is O(N log N). But heapify uses a bottom-up sift-down approach — most nodes are near the bottom (short sift distances). The total work sums to O(N) by the geometric series. Always use heapify when converting an existing list.

Lazy Deletion: Instead of removing arbitrary elements from a heap (no native support, would be O(N)), mark them as 'deleted' in a set and skip them when popped. This is how real schedulers handle cancellations. O(log N) per operation, O(1) extra per entry.

When to use a heap vs sorted list: Heap is better when you only need the min/max repeatedly (O(log N) per op). Sorted list (via bisect) is better when you need arbitrary rank queries or need to iterate in order. For a fully sorted stream, consider sortedcontainers.SortedList from PyPI.
